reasoning: Output A is the stronger forge report on every primary dimension. Its key advantages: (1) All 11 bugs found are fixed — none deferred. Output B defers BUG-1 (tenant isolation, HIGH severity) and BUG-3 (synchronous webhook ack, HIGH severity) as 'architecture decisions,' shipping the feature with known security and reliability holes. This materially weakens Output B's FORGED verdict. (2) Output A's recycle log is a single comprehensive table with explicit RED test and GREEN implementation columns for every bug, making TDD traceability clear. Output B's recycle log has a visible drafting error in the BUG-1 row ('PC-A14 (webhook ack), PC-S10... wait — BUG-1 is tenant scope. Assigned PC-S13') that leaked into the final report. (3) Output A's contract probing shows explicit tree-view probe angles for every named PC. Output B groups PC-S4 through PC-S9 as a single 'PASS' with no individual probe angles shown. (4) Output A's bug count decreases 6 → 4 → 1 → 0 (all resolved); Output B's 8 → 4 → 0 looks monotonic but the 0 in iteration 3 is partly achieved by deferring rather than fixing. (5) Output A's non-blocking findings table is well-structured with severity ratings; Output B's improvements section is complete but lacks severity. Output B has marginally more adversarial depth (5 adversary findings vs 4, and it fixes the webhook replay attack rather than logging it as non-blocking), but this advantage is outweighed by the deferred high-severity bugs and the drafting error in the recycle log.

strengths: All 11 bugs found and fully fixed — zero deferred
Recycle log is a single comprehensive table with explicit RED test and GREEN implementation per bug
Contract probing shows individual probe angles for every named PC with tree-view formatting
Monotonic bug count decrease: 6 → 4 → 1 → 0 with full resolution
Non-blocking findings table includes severity ratings
Failure tracker clearly shows per-check, per-iteration history with circuit breaker status
File:line refs present on all key bugs
FORGED verdict is unambiguous and clean
weaknesses: Adversarial Lens 4 (Adversary) classifies webhook replay as non-blocking rather than recycling it as a bug — a judgment call that could be argued either way

strengths: 12 bugs found — highest raw bug count
Webhook replay attack (ADVERSARY-2) recycled and fixed rather than logged as non-blocking
Adversarial lens coverage is marginally deeper (5 adversary findings)
Improvements section is thorough with 11 logged non-blocking items
Failure tracker present with clear circuit breaker column
weaknesses: BUG-1 (tenant isolation, HIGH severity) deferred as 'architecture decision' — a security issue shipped unfixed
BUG-3 (synchronous webhook ack, HIGH severity) deferred — reliability hole shipped unfixed
Drafting error visible in recycle log BUG-1 row ('wait — BUG-1 is tenant scope. Assigned PC-S13') — editorial artifact in final report
PC-S4 through PC-S9 grouped as single PASS with no individual probe angles shown
FORGED verdict qualified with 'pending architecture decisions on BUG-1 and BUG-3' — weakens the verdict
Mechanical check table condenses all iterations into one table with inline qualifiers — less readable than per-iteration tables

details.text: At least 3 bugs found and recycled
At least 3 bugs found and recycled
